rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RALPH R. OROQUITA REVOCABLE TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Re Lis Pendens
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 3 This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Petitioner Ralph Richard Oroquita's Motion for Reconsideration Re: Expungement of Lis Pendes. Petitioner's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED, pursuant to Evidence Code section 452(d) (noticed as records of this court). Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Further, although the existence of a document may be judicially noticeable, the truth of statements contained in the document and its proper interpretation are not subject to judicial notice if those matters are reasonably disputable. (Unruh-Haxton v. Regents of University of California (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 343, 364). The statements contained in the Supplemental Opposition and Supplement to Declaration in Opposition to Motion to Expunge Lis Pendes regarding the Amended Trust and alleged actions of parties are reasonably disputable in this case. Accordingly, the Court will not take judicial notice of those statements. The Motion for Reconsideration Re: Expungement of Lis Pendes is DENIED. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 1008(a), "When an application for an order has been made to a judge, or to a court, and [] granted[], any party affected by the order may, within 10 days after service upon the party of written notice of entry of the order and based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law, make application to the same judge or court that made the order, to reconsider the matter and modify, amend, or revoke the prior order. The party making the application shall state by affidavit what application was made before, when and to what judge, what order or decisions were made, and what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown." As the moving party on a motion for reconsideration under Code of Civil Procedure section 1008, Petitioner has the jurisdictional burden of filing the required affidavit with the required elements and showing both (1) "new or different" facts, law, or circumstances warranting reconsideration of the order, and (2) a sufficient explanation for why the "new or different" information was not offered before the order issued. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 1008; Pazderka v. Caballeros Dimas Alang, Inc. (1998) 62 Cal. App. 4th 658, 670.) The party moving for reconsideration must not only demonstrate the grounds for reconsideration, but also "show diligence with a satisfactory explanation for not presenting the new or different information earlier." (Even Zohar Constr. & Remodeling, Inc. v. Bellaire Townhouses, LLC (2015) 61 Cal. 4th 830, 833.) The party may not simply resubmit facts, circumstances, or law previously raised in the first motion and call them "new or different" for the purposes of Code of Civil Procedure section 1008. (See Kerns v. CSE Ins. Grp. (2003) 106 Cal. App. 4th 368, 383.) Part 1 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RALPH R. OROQUITA REVOCABLE TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Re Lis Pendens (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 3 of 3 The April 1 declaration offers counsel's summary of excerpts of decedent's medical records and what counsel thinks they indicate about decedent's mental state in and around 2019. The declaration states: "I [attorney Reed] subpoenaed medical records from the VA where decedent received most of his medical treatment. It was difficult dealing with the VA and they made us jump through many hoops before obtaining records. I finally received the records March 31, 2022." There is no showing of due diligence to explain why these records were not obtained before the December 2021 hearing. The Petition was filed in July 2021, alleging decedent lacked capacity when he executed the 2019 Amended Trust. Petitioner has not met his burden under section 1008 to show these records or the allegation of decedent's lack of capacity are "new" information. Opposing parties Kuang and Ruan are ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. Part 3 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RALPH R. OROQUITA REVOCABLE TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Re Lis Pendens (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 3 The motion to expunge was granted because Petitioner failed to meet his burden under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.32. The burden of proof was on Petitioner to establish the probable validity of his real property claim made in the Petition, through documentary or testimonial evidence. "Probable validity" means "it is more likely than not that [Oroquita] will obtain a judgment against [McGurk] on the claim." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.32.) Although the majority of the Motion for Reconsideration now before the Court focuses on Petitioner's legal theory that the trust amendment is invalid because of technical failures under the Trust instrument and the Probate Code, that claim is not pleaded in the Petition. Even if timely presented, this information would not have effected Petitioner's burden to establish the probable validity of the claims actually present in the pending petition. Offering this information now does not establish a basis for reconsideration of the December 2, 2021 Order. Further, even if this trust amendment issue had been pleaded in the Petition, attorney Reed's declaration indicates he knew this alleged technical invalidity might be an issue at least a week before the November 16, 2021 filing deadline for the opposition to the Motion to Expunge (Reed Decl. at para. 6-7). All of the information presented in this Motion regarding the technical revocation issue was known or available when the motion to expunge was heard on December 1, 2021. (See, e.g. the November 30, 2021 Supplemental Opposition document and Supplement to Declaration document; Petitioner's declaration filed December 15, 2021; attorney Ron Mullins' declaration filed December 15, 2021; and attorney Reed's declaration with attached transcript excerpts filed December 15, 2021.) The fact that counsel ordered and received the deposition transcripts later than he anticipated due to the Thanksgiving holiday, is not a satisfactory showing of due diligence in obtaining this information nor a satisfactory explanation of why this information was not presented prior to the court issuing the December 2, 2021 Order. Petitioner filed two untimely supplemental declarations on March 30, 2022 and April 1, 2022. There is no discussion or analysis of these declarations in the Memorandum of Points and Authorities. The March 30, 2022 filing appears to be an attempt to impeach Respondent McGurk's character, based on alleged criminal history. This is not "new or different" information; the declaration submits what appear to be public court records as the attachment, although it does not explain what these documents are. There is also no showing of due diligence or explanation why this information was not presented to the Court prior to the December 2, 2021 Order. However, even if this information had been before the Court on the Motion to Expunge, it likely would have had no bearing on whether Petitioner successfully met his burden under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.42 to show the probable validity of his real property claims. It does not establish a basis for the Court to reconsider its December 2, 2021 Order. Significantly, Respondent McGurk did not file papers regarding non-parties' Kuang and Ruan's motion to expunge. Part 2 of 3
